segments.text: Kira, thank you.
Can you hear me loud and clear? Yeah. Fantastic.
Hey, thank you for this opportunity.
I'd just like to acknowledge you, uh, in the challenge
that you have in front of you, listening to a lot
of people shouting at you at at, at high speed.
So, um, thank you for, for, uh, listening and look.
I'm the Chief Executive of Southland District Council.
Uh, we represent a large rural hazard exposed, uh, district,
approximately 20% of the south island,
and that requires both strong local capability
and effective national support.
Uh, the frequency and intensity
of extreme weather events has increased
exacerbating Southland's exposure to hazards.
We went for nearly 20 years without a state of emergency,
and then five were declared within six years.
Uh, in 2020, we had two flooding events,
2023 and gore.
There was an extensive flooding event.
2024, we had the blue cliffs, uh,
hazard, uh, coastal hazard.
And 2025, uh, there was the widespread, uh,
wind event.
Uh, we do support the core intent of the bill, uh,
being strengthened national leadership, better
iwis partnership, and improved risk reduction
and better recovery and welfare provisions.
However, while we are supportive of the intent,
we are concerned about its practical implementation.
Southern District Council needs a system that works
for small, rural and remote communities,
and the legislation must be practical,
resourced and flexible.
So the key messages that I would like to share with you, uh,
come under a couple of categories.
First is the system leadership and governance.
Uh, we would like to see that the relationships
between central and local government are clarified.
So those governance structures must clearly show
how emergency management committees interact with councils,
especially where funding, staffing,
and policy decisions, uh, intersect.
With respect to the partnership with Iwi Maori, we, um,
we strongly support that, uh, and, and consistent with TER
and South End District Council's existing, uh, relationship
and work with incorporated in ku.
However, the system needs to avoid duplicating structures
or creating confusion about representation.
So we request explicit flexibility,
so partnership structures reflect the local context
and the existing relationships.
We, we also would like
to see national versus regional versus local, uh, clarity
and support, clearer role delineation
and the use of the national lead agencies.
Um, operational overlap remains a concern, especially
how national direction interacts with local autonomy,
autonomy during fast moving events.
I also want to just touch on the chief executive duties,
which is a matter dear to my heart.
Uh, the council chief executive responsibilities
significantly expand under this bill, uh,
requiring the guidance
and creating pressure for small councils
where chief executives may also serve as controllers
or recovery managers.
And we also note that there is uncertainty in the transition
timing with some things being delayed, uh,
their commencement or key provisions being delayed, which,
uh, could well lead
to some uncertainty about when the responsibilities take
effect, the matter of implementation,
uh, the funding, the capability, and the capacity.
And this is one of the issues that, uh, is
of the greatest concern to us.
The new legislative requirements create substantial workload
and resourcing challenges,
and you will be keenly aware
that we are also facing some other challenges in terms
of other legislative demands
and, uh, controls on the amount that councils are allowed
to spend potentially.
So small and rural councils are already under great fiscal
pressure and, uh, risk of unfunded mandates,
uh, such as this one.
Uh, add to that complication, the risk reduction,
the reporting planning
and engagement duties assume at administrative scale,
many councils do not have.
So we request that central government funding
or an adjustment of obligations is made
to match a realistic capacity.
We do strongly support elevating risk reduction,
but risk assessments and resilient planning risks duplicates
work already underway in long-term plans
and spatial planning, climate adaptation, et cetera.
And it overlaps with the planning natural environment
and local government system improvements bills,
and it risks inconsistent expectations.
So we, we, like many others, I suspect in local government,
are requesting an alignment of the definitions of functions
and the processes across all of the related legislation.
The new concept of operational areas may create confusion in
regions like Southland with dispersed communities
and boundaries should generally follow existing structures
unless a strong case exists otherwise.
And we support increased powers for controllers,
but we, uh, urge accountability
to elected members must remain and it must remain clear.
And we would like to echo
Minister Mitchell's recent comments.
Uh, to that effect, shared controllers
and recovery managers must continue to be enabled
for small councils.
The community realities for a district like Southland, uh,
and disproportionately affected communities,
the bill uses a concept without explicitly defining it.
And so we would request a clear definition
that explicitly includes rural communities as well
as seniors, disabled children, culturally
and linguistically deser diverse communities,
and those experiencing socioeconomic deprivation,
the community and volunteer capacity.
Southland, rural Southland relies heavily on volunteers.
The recent event, uh, that we experienced across Southland
and Klu, uh, the, the loss of comms
and, uh, the, the lack
of any avail available electricity or support.
The areas like Tiano, which were incredibly hard hit,
mobilized themselves without communications and
provided support to community members.
But we, we do rely on volunteers.
So the bill needs to simplify
and should simplify response planning requirements
to better rec, recognize volunteering as a,
as is fundamental to resilience.
Um, and we also support inclusion of animal welfare,
but we note the increased engagement
and planning workload for rural districts
with varied livestock types.
So in closing, Southland District Council supports the
intent of the bill and is committed
to strengthening emergency management.
But to succeed, the new system must provide clear,
workable governance, recognize local capacity constraints,
avoid duplication across multiple reforms, and be fairly
and sustainably resourced, uh, and,
and, uh, deal with the inconsistencies
between various bits of legislation.
For example, the chief executive's responsibilities
under the local government act versus the chief executive's
responsibilities under this bill.
At the moment, we operate as many councils do.
We are required to be economic development agencies,
we're required to be infrastructure providers,
and we are required to be regulators.
We're also now being plundered by central government
to fund, uh, uh, the Commerce Commission
and tomato ROI, uh, we would note that
if this bill went in without having, um, suitable funding
to go with it, instead of providing all those services and,
and when required using our staff
to support emergency management,
we would in effect be setting up emergency management
systems that sometimes do infrastructure
and sometimes do community development
and sometimes do regulation. Thank you.

we see some challenges around just how

We now welcome Rashid,
I think it's Rashid from Gisborne District Council,
Rahi Rahi.
Apologies. Rahi.
There we go, RA, love. Lovely to see you. I'm Rahi Stoltz.
I'm the mayor at the Gisborne District Council,
and I have with me, um, our CEO Nadine Thatcher Swan,
and we have got a presentation of about seven minutes
that we would like to talk to you about, uh,
GIIN District Council as a unitary authority,
meaning we carry the full set of regional
and territorial responsibilities, including all
of emergency management costs.
Rafati covers over 8,000 square kilometers
with 270 kilometers of coastline.
Uh, we are sparsely populated, geographically isolated,
highly exposed to seism seismic tsunami flooding
and CLI climate driven, um, hazards.
And we have the highest proportion of Maori in any
of any region or in alto or as a unit three authority.
We cannot spread emergency management's costs
across multiple councils.
It is just us and increased statutory
responsibilities had a direct
and immediate impact on our rates.
This context underpinned many of our recommendations,
and it is critical when assessing the both practicality.
Um, I have lost count of
how many times I have declared civil defense
emergencies in our region.
So it is very close to our hearts here.
What we want to highlight to you,
our first major concern is alignment across legislation.
Local authorities are required
to manage hazard risk under multiple statuses,
including the C act, the local government act,
the Resource Management Act,
and the forthcoming coming climate and planning reforms.
All are currently being amended, all replaced.
This BO Bill places a strong emphasis on risk reduction,
but does not clearly differentiate risk reduction under
emergency management from risk reduction functions delivered
through planning infrastructure strategies
and managed retreat.
So for a unitary authority, this creates duplication,
inefficiency and extra cost, especially at a time
where more unitary authorities are being encouraged.
We are often required
to undertake multiple parallel hazard assessments using
different methodologies, standards, scopes,
and governments governance arrangements
to satisfy different legislative regimes.
Our submission supports stronger risk reduction,
however, the bill must clearly define
what risk reduction means in this context, ensure it aligns
with planning and infrastructure responsibilities under
other legislation
and require consistent methodologies and standards.
So councils are not forced to duplicate the same work across
multiple legal frameworks.
Without this, the bill risks increasing compliance burden
without improving outcomes, particularly for councils
with li with limited capacity.
Next, I wanna talk about central government support
and implementation.
The bill introduces new responsibilities beyond response,
including risk reduction, readiness and recovery,
but does not provide funding mechanisms for these functions
For unitary authorities,
emergency management costs sit largely in operating
expenditure and flow directly to rate payers
with constrained rating capacity
and proposed rate caps, there is a real risk
that statutory obligations will not be deliverable.
So our, um, submission seeks central government co-funding
for non-response emergency management functions,
particularly hazard data modeling and risk reduction,
and also the implementation support
through excessive accessible standards,
templates and guidance.
We also strongly support that any standards
or rules adopted under the bill must be freely accessible,
not not behind paid subscriptions,
which would shift national system costs onto councils
and communities.
Our fourth point is MANA participation,
structured and accountable.
Ang is an active and living partnership in
MAN plays essential governance, operational,
and community leadership roles in emergency management.
The bill strengthens Maori participation in some areas,
but limits mandated involvement at governance levels
to statutory roles with core coordinating executive groups,
which are not decision-making bodies.
Our submission six one discretion
for emergency management committees to invite MANA
to appointed to appoint representatives.
And two, the ability for MANA
to appoint their own representatives to the kick
or the coordinating executive groups.
For a unitary authority, it is critical
that participation is meaningful, it's structured, continues
and accountable rather than ad hoc.
The bull should enable locally appropriate arrangement
without imposing blanket or unworkable governance models.
My second to last point is localized declarations
for unitary authorities.
The bill intends to enable localized states of emergencies,
but as drafted, this does not work well
for unitary authorities that do not operate
with ward boundaries.
The legislation assumes district
or ward based geographic units,
but in practice, this constraints our ability
to make declarations that are proportionate
and targeted to the actual communities affected.
Our submission recommends explicitly enabling declarations
for defined localities, settlements, or geographic areas.
This is a practical change that would allow the bill
to function as intended across all local authority
structures, including our unitary authorities.
And then last but not least, permanent recovery powers
and national leadership.
Our, our final point, um, addresses recovery.
Our experience in demonstrates
that recovery is no longer an exceptional
or short term phase repeated
and compounding events have created prolonged recovery
environments that sit uncomfortably between response
and business as usual.
Regulation. The current approach relies on ad hoc
event specific legislation.
While effective this introduces delays, uncertainty,
and inconsistencies,
we recommend embedding a standing recovery framework within
the emergency management bill,
and also establishing a permanent national recovery entity,
or clearly designating a single lead agency
with responsibility to recovery, funding, coordination,
policy leadership, and system stewardship.
For unit three authorities managing prolonged recovery
with constrained capacity.
Permanent and predictable recovery arrangements are critical
to delivering timely
and equitable outcomes for our communities.
In closing, the Gman District Council supports the intent
of the bill, but seeks targeted amendments
to ensure it is workable for unit three authorities
and high risk unit, um, regions.
Our recommendations focus on clarity on integration,
affordability, and practical implementation.
We believe these changes will strengthen the emergency
management system and approve outcomes
for communities across Alt New Zealand.
We are happy to answer any questions if
you have any. Thank you.

Orion is the third largest

We have over 229,000 customer connections,
over 14,300 kilometers of lines and cables,

And we currently have an important role under the Civil

Our community especially depends on electricity during and
after high impact, low prop probability events such

And we will make our application in June this year.

with the 2023 bill.
So I just wanted to highlight a few

Uh, we are concerned about the widening of the duty

whereas the current position is
that every lifeline utility must ensure that it is able
to function to the fullest possible extent,
even though this may be at a reduced level during and
after an emergency.
The new duty is that we will need to ensure
that our infrastructure is able to function
to the fullest possible extent, et cetera.
So it seems to us that this will be of a higher standard
or a wider standard,
and it may be significantly harder to meet that,
given the definition of infrastructure components,

So infrastructure components means assets,
information networks, systems, suppliers,
people and processes.
So it is quite broad
and we ask that the select committee, um, consider reverting
to the previous formulation in section 60 of the
C-D-E-M-A.
Um, in terms of sector response plans,
Orion supports the development of sector response plans,

of emergency or a transition period.

how a sector response plan may address default information

We agree that information sharing is very important,
but it can present some legal difficulties

to contractual provisions, privacy,
and other considerations.
And we also need to be assured
that Orion's information will be treated
with the necessary protections.
Another provision we would like
to see clarified is clause 130 of the bill.
Clause one 30 sets out various powers to close roads
and public places during an emergency.
We support this clause,
but we would like to see clear provision
for allowing certain persons
or groups of persons into restricted areas.
We need to be able to get our authorized staff
and contractors to our network quickly
to affect repairs while also maintaining safety.
And we think the bill would benefit from this clarification.
And we have some suggested wording here.
We have made some comments on the compliance provisions in

to regimes under the Commerce Act

to reconsider the change to the duty

and developing sustainably
and sensibly managing natural hazard risks supports new
Zealand's access to reinsurance, a key driver
of insurance accessibility.
And finally taking a broader view of all policy decisions,
building or at least supporting resilience
and natural hazards should be considered across all
government policies.
Even those with objectives not
directly aimed at managing risks.
Insurers strongly support growth and development
and we suggest that, for example,
policy is aimed at enabling growth
and future development should adopt a do no
harm approach to resilience.
Avoiding one step forward and two steps back.
Ultimately through growth that takes natural hazard risk
into account and proactively manages that risk.
We protect communities
and the insurance market remains accessible
and sustainable for the long term.
In summary, the insurance
and emergency management sectors are aligned
and we believe that working together will improve
future recovery outcomes.
At the same time as we prepare for future events,
we encourage the government to systematically manage those
natural hazard risks to reduce the impact of those events.
Um, thanks for the time today.
I'm happy to to leave my remaining time
for any questions you may have on our submission.
Thank you very much, Sean. That was excellent.
Um, I'll just look around the other members.
Looks like Andy Foster is up first. Uh,
Look, thank you very much Sean for that.
Just, um, we've had quite a number of submissions.
It's something I'm very, um, uh,
very interested in the whole relationship with planning, uh,
between emergency management planning so that we try
and minimize risk right up front.
The reduction part, um, the,
you've got a suggestion in your submission about
particularly around data and information sharing.
What do you see the role of, um,
of the insurance sector being in the planning process in
basically cutting off, um, development in in places,
which we will put it at high risk.
Um, and also you've also
talked about sharing of information.
I've got a, a bill actually around specifically sharing
of information for customers, um, or prospective customers.
So I'm just interested in the transparency of that, um,
and to who that transparency is, uh, is being suggested.
So two parts of the question.
Yeah, I I think, um, when it comes to information sharing,
there, there are two sides of it when we talk about it, um,
from an insurance industry angle, um,
our submission deal specifically with information post-event
and sharing information from the emergency management sector
to insurers post-event to enable them to plan
and resource and scale.
Um, it sounds like your question deals more with that kind
of planning and future risk. It
It's be it's before event
so we don't have the event in the first place. Yes.
Yeah, exactly. And while we would be supportive of that,
I think largely this sector wants
to participate in those planning conversations.
Um, it can be a difficult question to answer
because often at the end of the day, the, um,
the insurance industry answer is that any consideration
for an offering of insurance is on a case by case basis.
Um, and so it's hard to answer broad questions about,
for example, will this subdivision be insurable?
Um, there are a lot of questions that need to happen
underneath that, um, to, to determine how it will be built
to what standard, um, finish floor heights,
what mitigation is in place if it is a
community that would be exposed.
Um, so that is a difficult question to answer.
However, saying that, um, some of my colleagues at ICNZ
and other people in the industry have been working with, um,
MFE through some of the RMA reforms
and have engaged quite deeply on these questions.
I think ultimately, um, if I had to answer right now, today,
the sector wants to be involved in those conversations,
at least to give some qualitative advice on
how insurers view some of those risks, um,
to hopefully inform some of those planning decisions.
Would that in insureability question in mind,
But so at least while you would say you'd, you'd need
to have more information, do things on a case by case basis
for individual properties, you would presumably
as an industry be able to look at a particular,
a wider environment and say, look, that's,
that's an environment which gives us cause
for concern or it doesn't.
Um, yeah, I think a, again,
in a qualitative sense it would be easy
to say there's a location
or a, a wider area where there's a cause
for concern from an insurance perspective,
but in many cases it's important to remember that there,
there can be measures put in place to mitigate the risk that
An area, I understand that at least it triggers those,
at least it triggers those measures rather than
them ignoring them.
Sure. Yep. Okay. No, that's helpful. Thank you.
Thank you. And uh, thank you Sean
for your time on behalf of the council today.
We, we appreciate it
and the thorough written submission as well.
Um, members, I'm sure we'll be, uh, keen
to hear from Disability Connect when we return.
They are not scheduled to join us now until three o'clock,
so we'll take a slight break 'cause it's been a long day.
Can I just briefly say to the committee,
and you can be off the live stream if you want, but.
